items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.

items.q: What is the first step of formal analysis?
items.correct: Description - noting what is actually present in the work.
items.why: Students jump to meaning immediately. Formal analysis follows a sequence: describe, analyse formal elements, interpret, evaluate. Description grounds interpretation in what's actually there.

items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.q: Who developed iconological method in Studies in Iconology 1939?
items.why: Correct answer: Erwin Panofsky. “Heinrich Wolfflin” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1998 Art and Agency treats artworks as social agents?
items.why: Correct answer: Gell. “Bourdieu” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many screens does the HL Comparative Study require maximum?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 screens. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
